title: Chapter 31: The Aftermath
content: Six weeks.

That's the measure of everything now. Not days — days are too granular, too hopeful, each one pretending it might carry something new. Weeks are honest. Six of them since the fire. Since Jax and Vivienne vanished into the algorithmic dark. Since my life stopped being mine and became evidence.

I sit in a coffee shop on Santa Monica Boulevard that I can actually afford, and I write. The brew here is bitter in a way that clings to the back of your throat — burnt, recycled, the kind of coffee that knows it's cheap and resents you for drinking it anyway. But it's four dollars a cup and the Wi-Fi doesn't require a password, and the barista doesn't make small talk, and sometimes that's all you need from a place.

The laptop is the same brand. The manuscript document has the same filename — *gilded_glitch_v4.docx* — though the version number has climbed higher than I can keep track of. But everything else is gone. The Malibu cottage with its morning fog and the sound of waves that Jax used to call "the only honest thing in Los Angeles." The documentary footage, seized by the FBI three weeks ago under a subpoena so broad it might as well have been a net. The security deposit I'll never see because the landlord claimed "water damage" which was really just the ocean doing what the ocean does to houses that aren't meant for people like me.

I couldn't afford Malibu without Jax's rent subsidy. That's the plain truth of it, and I've stopped being embarrassed by plain truths. Jax split that cottage with me not because I asked but because she could, and because she wanted me close, and because the world she built had room for me in it. The world she left behind does not.
I take another sip of the burnt coffee and keep writing.
*Jax Vance didn't disappear. She executed. The difference matters.*
That's the opening line I've rewritten fourteen times. It's still not right. But nothing is right, so I keep typing anyway.
Tom Hargrove is finished.

I don't use that word lightly — I know what it means to be the person whose life is summarized in passive voice by strangers. But "finished" is the only word that fits. The affair — if you can call years of predation an affair — unspooled on national television the way these things do, in fragments at first and then all at once, like a sweater pulled by a loose thread. Cassandra's testimony cracked it open. She sat in that courtroom in a blazer two sizes too big — borrowed from Mateo's closet, I'd later learn — and told the truth in a voice that never wavered. Not once. Not even when Hargrove's lawyer tried to paint her as consenting, ambitious, a opportunist who traded favors for access.

She was magnificent. I wasn't there — the FBI had already confiscated my footage and strongly suggested I stay away from the proceedings — but I watched the transcript the way I watch everything now: obsessively, alone, at two in the morning.
Cassandra opened the door. Six more women walked through it.

Hargrove's political career didn't end — it disintegrated. His endorsements evaporated overnight. His party issued a statement that used the word "disappointed" seven times, which is the maximum number of times politicians will express disappointment before they actually mean it. The Bel Air mansion is being sold as part of the divorce settlement, negotiated by a woman named Harriet Vance who took Vivienne's case pro bono and has, according to the court filings I've memorized like scripture, "pursued maximum equitable distribution with extreme prejudice."

I didn't know Vivienne had hired new counsel. I didn't know much about Vivienne at all anymore — whether she was safe, whether she was the one who'd planned the exit or the one who'd been carried along by it. The letter Jax sent through Mateo said *We're safe. Don't look for us.* But "we" could mean Jax and Vivienne, or it could mean Jax and the ghost of who Vivienne used to be.

Hargrove lives in a hotel now. The Meridian on Wilshire, mid-tier business accommodations, the kind of place where the lobby smells like industrial cleaner and polite desperation. I know this because Agent Reyes mentioned it during our last "interview" — not to inform me, but to let me know she was keeping track of all the pieces, even the broken ones.
Cassandra is okay.

I need to write that sentence and then sit with it, because "okay" is such a small word for what she's become. She enrolled in a community college nursing program three weeks ago — evening classes, anatomy and physiology, the kind of prerequisites that stack toward a future she never thought she'd have. The program is funded by an anonymous scholarship. The financial aid office has no record of the donor. The endowment was processed through a series of intermediaries that, I'm certain, trace back to a blockchain that no longer officially exists.
Jax.

She set it up before the glitch. Before the fire. Before she became a ghost in a system she invented. She looked at Cassandra Rivera — who'd been used and discarded by men with power — and she built her a bridge she'd never have to cross alone.

Mateo's garage is stable too. Three months of rent, paid in advance, from the same invisible source. No name, no paper trail, just money that appeared in the right accounts at the right time. Mateo doesn't know where it came from. He suspects, the way he suspects most things — quietly, without asking, with the particular silence of a man who understands that some gifts come with gratitude as their only acceptable currency.
Cassandra and Mateo are survivors. They're okay. They're rebuilding, brick by careful brick, in a world that tried to grind them to dust and failed.
I'm happy for them. I am. But happiness, right now, has the texture of watching someone eat a meal you can't afford — nourishing to witness, impossible to taste.
The manuscript is the only thing I have left of her.

Not her — not her voice on the phone at midnight, not her hands on my waist in the Malibu kitchen, not the way she said my name like it was code she was still debugging. The letter came once. Six weeks of silence since.

I write about her because I can't reach her. I build her in sentences the way she built worlds in code — line by line, function by function, each word a tiny executable that does something when someone reads it. She could write a program that moved millions of dollars in milliseconds. I write paragraphs that take hours and might move one person, someday, if I'm lucky.

Code is fast. Words are slow. I feel the delay in my bones, in the stiffness of my wrists after a long session, in the way the page count inches upward while the world spins on without waiting. Jax changed everything with a keystroke. I'm trying to change something with a sentence, and the gulf between those two acts is so vast it makes me laugh sometimes — sharp, humorless, into my bad coffee.

But I keep writing. Because the alternative is silence, and silence is what they want. The FBI wants me quiet. Hargrove, in his hotel room, wants me quiet. The whole machinery of the world that Jax glitched wants me to write the ending they've already chosen — the one where the glitch was a crime and the woman who executed it was a villain.
I'm writing a different ending.

The coffee shop door opens and the Santa Monica breeze cuts through, salt-thick and warm, carrying the faintest smell of the ocean I can no longer afford to live beside. A woman in a linen shirt enters, orders something complicated, leaves. The barista wipes down the counter. I type another sentence. The afternoon light shifts.
This is my life now. This is the aftermath.
The P.O. box key is cold in my hand.

I've been checking it every day — a small metal box in a strip mall on Main Street, the kind of place that also sells packing tape and passport photos and the quiet desperation of people who can't receive mail at a fixed address. The key is worn smooth on one side from use, the ridges soft under my thumb the way a worry stone softens over time.
Today, there's something inside.

A package. Small, padded, wrapped in plain brown paper. No return address. The postmark is smudged beyond legibility — could be anywhere, could be nowhere. I hold it for a long time before I open it. My heart does something it hasn't done in six weeks. It hopes.
I tear the paper.
Inside: a cold wallet.

Not the old one — not the Trezor that Jax carried in her jacket like a talisman, the one that held the keys to the glitch. This is new. Different model, different shape. Sleeker, black, with a small OLED screen that flickers to life the moment I lift it from the packaging.
A single file is stored on it.
Coordinates.

South Pacific. I know the format — Jax taught me to read latitude and longitude the way other people read poetry, with reverence and the occasional confusion. These coordinates point to an island. I don't know which one yet, but I know the region. French Polynesia. The kind of place where money buys invisibility and the nearest jurisdiction is a twelve-hour boat ride away.
And below the coordinates, a line of green code:
The screen blinks. Green. Soft, rhythmic, like a pulse. Like a heartbeat measured in light.
Viv. July 12. Vivienne's birthday. Jax's signal — embedded in code that only I would read as language, only I would understand as a name wrapped in a date wrapped in a declaration.
*Vivienne is safe. I am safe. Come find us.*
The signal isn't dead.

I stand outside the strip mall and the Santa Ana wind pushes through my hair, warm and dry and smelling of sage and distant wildfire, and I hold the cold wallet in my palm and feel its weight — not the physical weight, which is negligible, barely the heft of a credit card, but the gravitational weight of everything it represents.
A lifeline. A door left open. Jax — alive, reaching across six weeks of silence with a line of green code that says *I haven't stopped thinking about you.*
And a trap.

Because Agent Reyes is watching me. She hasn't said so explicitly — she doesn't have to. The unmarked sedan that appears three cars behind mine when I drive to the grocery store. The "routine follow-up" calls that always come when I deviate from my usual pattern. The way she asked about the P.O. box last week, casual as weather chatter: *Do you receive mail at a fixed address, Ms. Chen, or do you use an alternative?*
She knows. Maybe not about this wallet — not yet — but she knows there's a channel. She's waiting for me to use it.

If I follow the signal — if I book a flight, if I trace those coordinates, if I let the green code lead me to wherever Jax and Vivienne are hiding — I lead the FBI straight to them. Every search query, every reservation, every credit card swipe. The machinery that Jax glitched is the same machinery that the FBI uses to track people who glitch the machinery. There is no irony in this. There is only the math of surveillance, and the math says: Maya Chen moves, and Reyes follows.

But if I don't go — if I bury the wallet in a drawer and keep writing my slow words in my cheap coffee shop and let the coordinates sit unopened on a device I never plug in — then the signal dies. The door closes. And I am alone in the aftermath forever.
The wind picks up. The green code blinks in my hand, patient as a lighthouse.
*Come find us.*
I close my fingers around the wallet.

I don't move yet. I don't breathe. I stand in the Santa Monica strip mall parking lot, six weeks after the end of the world, and I hold the only proof I have that the people I love are still alive, and I try to calculate the distance between a lifeline and a trap.
The math won't resolve.
The code keeps blinking.
